1:
Ps 51: The Miserere — The Penitential Psalm par excellence. The superscription links the psalm to 2 Sam 11-12: David's sin with Bathsheba (adultery, complicity in Uriah's death) and Nathan's confrontation ('you are the man'). The historical occasion grounds the psalm's universality: if David — the man after God's own heart, the author of the Psalter — requires this depth of confession, so does every person. The psalm became the primary penitential text of both Jewish liturgy (morning prayers) and Christian practice (Ash Wednesday, the Daily Office, pre-Communion confession).

'Have mercy on me, O God, according to your steadfast love' — chonneni El kechasdecha — the petition is grounded in divine character, not in any claim the petitioner can make. Chesed (steadfast/covenant love) and rachame (compassion/womb-love, plural) are the two divine attributes invoked. The plural 'according to the abundance of your compassion' is a quantity modifier: the sin is vast; the compassion must be correspondingly vast.
2: 'Wash me thoroughly from my iniquity, and cleanse me from my sin' — two purification metaphors: kabbes (wash thoroughly, the laundry/fulling word — beating and scrubbing cloth clean) and tahar (make ritually pure, the Levitical cleanness term). The first is intense domestic cleaning; the second is ritual/covenantal restoration. Both are needed: the sin is simultaneously a moral stain requiring thorough removal and a ritual impurity requiring Levitical purification procedures. Cf. Lev 14 (purification of skin disease) and Num 19 (purification from corpse contamination).
3: 'For I know my transgressions, and my sin is ever before me' — the confession of full self-knowledge. The three sin-words in vv1-3: pesha (willful rebellion/breach of covenant), avon (iniquity/moral crookedness), chata (sin, missing the mark). All three appear again in v2, making Ps 51 a comprehensive survey of Israel's sin-vocabulary. 'Ever before me' — the inability to escape the awareness of the sin: it is constantly visible in the conscience.
4: 'Against you, you only, have I sinned and done what is evil in your sight' — lecha levadecha chatati (against you, you alone, have I sinned). This verse has generated sustained theological debate: David sinned against Bathsheba and Uriah, so how 'only' against God? The answer is covenantal: all sin is ultimately a breach of the covenant relationship with YHWH, who is the source of all moral obligation. 'So that you are justified in your words and blameless in your judgment' — YHWH's verdict is accepted before it is pronounced. Paul cites this in Rom 3:4 to establish YHWH's judicial rectitude.
5: 'Behold, I was brought forth in iniquity, and in sin did my mother conceive me' — hen beaven cholalti (behold, in iniquity I was formed/writhed). This verse has been foundational in the Western church's theology of original sin (Augustine, the Reformers). The grammatical subject is the psalmist, not the mother: it is not a claim about maternal sin during conception but about the structural moral condition of the human person from the earliest moment of existence. Comparison: Job 14:4 ('who can bring clean from unclean?'); Gen 6:5 ('every intention of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually'). The verse does not celebrate this condition but confesses it as the ground for the radical, divine intervention the psalm requests.
6: 'Behold, you delight in truth in the inward being, and you teach me wisdom in the secret heart' — emet batuchot (truth in the inner parts) — YHWH desires truth in the most hidden interior, not external compliance. The 'secret heart' (satom, the concealed/hidden place) is where wisdom must be taught. ANE temple religion focused on visible acts of sacrifice and ritual; the Psalter (especially the penitential psalms) insists that YHWH's real demand is interior transformation. This is the ground for the 'create in me a clean heart' petition of v10.
7: 'Purge me with hyssop, and I shall be clean; wash me, and I shall be whiter than snow' — techateni (purge me with hyssop) — hyssop (ezov) was the small, bushy plant used to apply the purification blood and water in Levitical rituals: the purification of skin disease (Lev 14:4-7), the red heifer purification from corpse contamination (Num 19:18). Jesus is offered wine on hyssop at the cross (John 19:29). 'Whiter than snow' — snow as the superlative of whiteness and purity, used in Isa 1:18 for the transformation of scarlet sins to white.
8: 'Let me hear joy and gladness; let the bones that you have broken rejoice' — 'the bones you have broken' — the physical experience of divine judgment as broken bones: the intensity of the guilt as a skeletal fracturing. The request to 'hear joy and gladness' is a request that the hearing-organs, previously afflicted by the sound of divine judgment, now receive the sound of rescue. ANE lament texts similarly describe illness as the crushing of bones and the healing as the restoration of normal function.
9: 'Hide your face from my sins, and blot out all my iniquities' — the divine face-hiding request: in v7-9 the psalmist asks for YHWH not to see the sin. Macheh (blot out, erase from a record) — the cancellation of a written debt or legal record. ANE contracts were written on clay tablets; debt cancellation was literally the erasure of the tablet. YHWH's ledger of sins is requested to be erased.
10: 'Create in me a clean heart, O God, and renew a right spirit within me' — bara li lev tahor (create for me a clean heart). Bara (create) is the Genesis 1 term, used exclusively of divine action, the creation from nothing or the transformation of something categorically new. The request is for a moral creation event: YHWH must create ex nihilo a clean heart, because the existing heart is not renewable by repair — it needs to be made new. This is the most theologically radical verse of the psalm and anticipates the new covenant promise of Jer 31:31-34 and Ezek 36:26 ('I will give you a new heart').
11: 'Cast me not away from your presence, and take not your Holy Spirit from me' — ruach qodshecha (your Holy Spirit) — one of only three occurrences of 'Holy Spirit' in the OT (Isa 63:10-11). In the context of 2 Sam 16:14 (the Spirit departed from Saul), David's prayer reflects the awareness that YHWH's Spirit can be withdrawn from those who fall from covenant faithfulness. The 'presence' (panecha) and the 'Holy Spirit' are parallel: the Spirit is the mode of divine presence. This verse became central to pneumatological discussion in the NT era.
12: 'Restore to me the joy of your salvation, and uphold me with a willing spirit' — ruach nedivah (a willing/noble/generous spirit) — nadiv (willing, the freewill-offering word from nadav) — the spirit characterized by voluntary, generous responsiveness rather than coerced compliance. The renewed spirit is freely willing, not mechanically obedient. This is the Jeremiah/Ezekiel new covenant anthropology: the law written on the heart produces willing obedience, not legal compulsion.
13: 'Then I will teach transgressors your ways, and sinners will return to you' — the restored sinner becomes the teacher of other sinners. The logic of testimony: personal experience of divine forgiveness becomes the most persuasive evidence for others. The psalm that began with individual confession (v3) now projects communal impact: the psalmist's restored relationship will generate the conversion of others. This is the same dynamic as John 4 (the Samaritan woman's testimony) and Ps 22:22-31 (the individual rescue becomes communal praise).
14: 'Deliver me from bloodguiltiness, O God, O God of my salvation, and my tongue will sing aloud of your righteousness' — damim (bloodguilt, bloods plural) — the specific sin of Uriah's death. The plural 'bloods' in Hebrew law referred to the guilt of multiple blood-related deaths, including not only the killer but those who arranged or enabled the killing. David arranged Uriah's death through Joab; the multiple layers of guilt are captured in the plural. 'My tongue will sing' — praise is the result of forgiveness, not the condition for it.
15: 'O Lord, open my lips, and my mouth will declare your praise' — the lips must be divinely opened for praise: the guilty mouth cannot offer praise on its own initiative. This petition became the opening prayer of the Daily Office in Christian liturgy: 'O Lord, open my lips, and my mouth shall declare your praise' (the Versicle before morning prayer). The guilt of the lips (they participated in the conspiracy of silence, vv3-5) must be released by divine action before they can speak praise.
16: 'For you will not delight in sacrifice, or I would give it; you will not be pleased with a burnt offering' — the Asaph/Ps 50 theme within Ps 51: sacrifice is not what YHWH wants most. This verse has a precise historical setting: after the murder of Uriah, David could offer no sacrifice to undo the sin — no animal payment could cover premeditated murder. The psalm therefore reaches for something deeper than the sacrificial system: the interior transformation of vv10-12 is what no temple sacrifice can accomplish.
17: 'The sacrifices of God are a broken spirit; a broken and contrite heart, O God, you will not despise' — zivche Elohim ruach nishbarah (the sacrifices of God are a broken spirit) — the paradox: the sacrifice that YHWH actually desires is the crushed, humbled person. Nishbarah (broken, shattered) + nidkeh (crushed, contrite) — two words for the complete demolition of self-sufficiency. YHWH does not despise this offering. The broken person offering the broken spirit is precisely what the whole sacrificial system was designed to express and effect — now articulated in its purest, interiorized form.
18: 'Do good to Zion in your good pleasure; build up the walls of Jerusalem' — vv18-19 are widely regarded as a later addition, possibly exilic or post-exilic, redirecting the individual penitential psalm toward communal restoration. 'Build up the walls of Jerusalem' implies that the walls are broken — consistent with the post-586 BC context. The individual's confession is extended to national confession and restoration. This addition transforms the psalm into the prayer of the exile community: just as David needed forgiveness and restoration, so does exiled Israel.
19: 'Then you will delight in right sacrifices, in burnt offerings and whole burnt offerings; then bulls will be offered on your altar' — the eschatological restoration of sacrifice after communal renewal. The polemic of vv16-17 (against hollow sacrifice) is not a permanent abolition of sacrifice but a diagnosis of current inauthenticity: when the interior condition is right (v17, the broken and contrite heart), the exterior sacrifices become genuine expressions of that condition. Bulls on the restored altar express the restored community's authentic covenant worship.
